# Baker 1031 Investments

> Founder-led real-estate securities firm helping accredited investors defer capital-gains tax through 1031 exchanges into institutional Delaware Statutory Trust (DST) properties, 721 UPREIT exchanges, Opportunity Zone funds, and mineral royalties. All educational content below is written by Gerald F. "Jerry" Baker, III and reviewed for compliance.

Securities are offered through Aurora Securities, Inc. (member FINRA/SIPC — BrokerCheck CRD #46147). This is educational information, not an offer, recommendation, or tax/legal advice; consult your own CPA and attorney.

Contact: Baker 1031 Investments, 650 California Street, San Francisco, CA 94108 · +1 (415) 579-1660 · invest@baker1031.com

## Learn library (with summaries)

- [Who Should Invest in Opportunity Zone Funds?](https://baker1031.com/learn/who-should-invest-in-opportunity-zone-funds/): Opportunity Zone funds offer powerful tax benefits, but they aren't right for everyone. This guide profiles the investors who fit — those with a big capita

## Glossary (with definitions)

- [10-Year Hold](https://baker1031.com/glossary/10-year-hold/): Holding a Qualified Opportunity Fund investment for at least 10 years lets an investor eliminate capital gains tax on the fund's own appreciation when sold.
- [1031 Exchange](https://baker1031.com/glossary/1031-exchange/): A 1031 exchange lets a real estate investor defer capital gains tax by reinvesting sale proceeds from one investment property into another like-kind property.
- [180-Day Exchange Period](https://baker1031.com/glossary/180-day-exchange-period/): The 180-day exchange period is the deadline in a 1031 exchange by which the investor must close on replacement property, 180 calendar days from the sale.
- [45-Day Identification Period](https://baker1031.com/glossary/45-day-identification-period/): The 45-day identification period is the window in a 1031 exchange during which the investor must formally identify potential replacement properties in writing.
- [721 Exchange](https://baker1031.com/glossary/721-exchange/): A 721 exchange lets an investor contribute real estate to a REIT's operating partnership in exchange for OP units, deferring capital gains tax under Section 721.
- [Accredited Investor](https://baker1031.com/glossary/accredited-investor/): An accredited investor is a person or entity that meets SEC income or net worth thresholds, allowing them to buy private, unregistered securities like DSTs.
- [Accredited Investor vs. Qualified Purchaser](https://baker1031.com/glossary/accredited-investor-vs-qualified-purchaser/): Accredited investor and qualified purchaser are two SEC investor tiers; a qualified purchaser meets a much higher bar, generally $5 million in investments.
- [Adjusted Basis](https://baker1031.com/glossary/adjusted-basis/): Adjusted basis is your original cost in a property increased by improvements and reduced by depreciation, used to calculate taxable gain when you sell.
- [Beneficial Interest](https://baker1031.com/glossary/beneficial-interest/): A beneficial interest is an investor's fractional ownership share in a Delaware Statutory Trust, entitling them to income and appreciation without holding title.
- [Boot](https://baker1031.com/glossary/boot/): Boot is any cash or non-like-kind value received in a 1031 exchange; it is taxable up to the amount of gain and reduces the deferral you achieve.
- [Cap Rate](https://baker1031.com/glossary/cap-rate/): Cap rate, or capitalization rate, is a property's annual net operating income divided by its price, expressing the unleveraged yield as a percentage.
- [Capital Gains Tax](https://baker1031.com/glossary/capital-gains-tax/): Capital gains tax is the federal tax on profit from selling an asset like real estate; long-term gains are taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income.
- [Cost Basis](https://baker1031.com/glossary/cost-basis/): Cost basis is what you originally paid for a property, including purchase price and acquisition costs, and it is the starting point for calculating gain.
- [Debt Replacement](https://baker1031.com/glossary/debt-replacement/): Debt replacement is the 1031 rule that an investor must take on debt on the replacement property equal to the mortgage paid off, or add cash, to defer all tax.
- [Delaware Statutory Trust](https://baker1031.com/glossary/delaware-statutory-trust/): A Delaware Statutory Trust is a legal entity that holds real estate and lets multiple investors own fractional, passive, 1031-eligible interests in the property.
- [Depreciation Recapture](https://baker1031.com/glossary/depreciation-recapture/): Depreciation recapture is the tax owed on gain attributable to depreciation deductions previously taken on a property, taxed at up to 25% for real estate.
- [Distribution Yield](https://baker1031.com/glossary/distribution-yield/): Distribution yield is the annual income a DST or REIT pays investors, expressed as a percentage of the amount invested, a key measure of cash flow.
- [Drop and Swap](https://baker1031.com/glossary/drop-and-swap/): A drop and swap is a technique where a partnership distributes property to partners as tenants in common before a 1031 exchange so each can exchange separately.
- [DST vs. TIC](https://baker1031.com/glossary/dst-vs-tic/): DST vs. TIC compares two fractional real estate structures for 1031 exchanges; DSTs allow many passive investors while TICs cap owners at 35 with more control.
- [FFO (Funds From Operations)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/ffo/): Funds from operations (FFO) is a REIT earnings measure that adds depreciation back to net income, giving a truer picture of cash generated by the properties.
- [Full-Cycle](https://baker1031.com/glossary/full-cycle/): Full-cycle describes a DST or non-traded REIT investment that has completed its life span, acquired, operated, and sold, returning capital to investors.
- [Improvement Exchange](https://baker1031.com/glossary/improvement-exchange/): An improvement exchange lets a 1031 investor use exchange funds to build or renovate the replacement property before taking title, counting the work toward value.
- [Like-Kind Property](https://baker1031.com/glossary/like-kind-property/): Like-kind property refers to real estate held for investment or business use that can be exchanged tax-deferred under Section 1031; nearly all real estate qualifies.
- [Master Lease](https://baker1031.com/glossary/master-lease/): A master lease is an arrangement where a DST leases its entire property to a master tenant, who operates it and pays rent used to fund investor distributions.
- [Mortgage Boot](https://baker1031.com/glossary/mortgage-boot/): Mortgage boot is the taxable gain created in a 1031 exchange when the debt on the replacement property is less than the debt paid off on the sold property.
- [NAV (Net Asset Value)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/nav/): Net asset value (NAV) is the per-share value of a fund or REIT, total assets minus liabilities divided by shares, used to price shares and track worth.
- [Net Investment Income Tax](https://baker1031.com/glossary/net-investment-income-tax/): The Net Investment Income Tax is a 3.8% federal surtax on investment income for higher earners, applying to rental income, capital gains, dividends, and more.
- [OP Units](https://baker1031.com/glossary/op-units/): OP units are operating partnership units received when contributing property to a REIT's partnership in a 721 exchange, deferring tax while paying distributions.
- [Opportunity Zone](https://baker1031.com/glossary/opportunity-zone/): An Opportunity Zone is an IRS-designated distressed area where investing capital gains through a Qualified Opportunity Fund earns tax deferral and other breaks.
- [Passive Income](https://baker1031.com/glossary/passive-income/): Passive income is earnings from investments requiring little ongoing effort, such as DST distributions or rental income, and is taxed under special IRS rules.
- [PPM (Private Placement Memorandum)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/ppm/): A private placement memorandum is the detailed disclosure document a sponsor gives investors in a private offering like a DST, outlining terms, risks, and fees.
- [Qualified Intermediary](https://baker1031.com/glossary/qualified-intermediary/): A qualified intermediary is an independent third party that holds 1031 exchange proceeds and handles the paperwork so the investor never touches the cash.
- [Qualified Intermediary Bond](https://baker1031.com/glossary/qualified-intermediary-bond/): A qualified intermediary bond is a fidelity bond protecting a 1031 investor's exchange funds against loss from theft or misappropriation by the intermediary.
- [Qualified Opportunity Fund](https://baker1031.com/glossary/qualified-opportunity-fund/): A Qualified Opportunity Fund is an investment vehicle that pools capital gains and invests at least 90% of assets in Opportunity Zone property or businesses.
- [Qualified Opportunity Zone Business](https://baker1031.com/glossary/qualified-opportunity-zone-business/): A qualified opportunity zone business is an operating business meeting IRS tests that a Qualified Opportunity Fund can invest in to satisfy its 90% asset rule.
- [Realized Gain vs. Recognized Gain](https://baker1031.com/glossary/realized-gain-vs-recognized-gain/): Realized gain is the total profit on a property sale; recognized gain is the portion actually taxed now, and a 1031 exchange defers recognition of that gain.
- [Regulation D (Reg D)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/regulation-d/): Regulation D is the SEC framework letting companies raise capital by selling securities privately to investors without full SEC registration, used for most DSTs.
- [Regulation D Rule 506(b)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/regulation-d-rule-506b/): Rule 506(b) is a Regulation D exemption allowing unlimited capital from accredited investors plus up to 35 non-accredited ones, but bars general solicitation.
- [Regulation D Rule 506(c)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/regulation-d-rule-506c/): Rule 506(c) is a Regulation D exemption allowing public advertising of a private offering, but all investors must be accredited and their status verified.
- [REIT](https://baker1031.com/glossary/reit/): A REIT is a company that owns or finances income-producing real estate and must pay out at least 90% of taxable income to shareholders as dividends.
- [Replacement Property](https://baker1031.com/glossary/replacement-property/): Replacement property is the new like-kind real estate a 1031 investor acquires with exchange proceeds to complete the exchange and defer capital gains tax.
- [Reverse Exchange](https://baker1031.com/glossary/reverse-exchange/): A reverse exchange lets a 1031 investor buy the replacement property before selling the relinquished one, with an accommodation titleholder parking the new asset.
- [Securitized Real Estate](https://baker1031.com/glossary/securitized-real-estate/): Securitized real estate refers to property ownership packaged as securities, like DST interests or REIT shares, that investors buy instead of holding title.
- [Sponsor](https://baker1031.com/glossary/sponsor/): A sponsor is the company that structures, acquires, and manages a DST or real estate offering, sourcing the property and running it on investors' behalf.
- [Springing LLC](https://baker1031.com/glossary/springing-llc/): A springing LLC is a standby structure that converts a DST into an LLC if the trust hits financial trouble, letting a new manager take active control.
- [Step-Up in Basis](https://baker1031.com/glossary/step-up-in-basis/): Step-up in basis resets an inherited asset's cost basis to its fair market value at the owner's death, potentially erasing deferred capital gains for heirs.
- [Tenants in Common (TIC)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/tenants-in-common-tic/): Tenancy in common is a co-ownership structure where multiple investors hold direct deeded shares of a property, each able to use their share in a 1031 exchange.
- [Triple-Net Lease (NNN)](https://baker1031.com/glossary/triple-net-lease-nnn/): A triple-net lease is a lease where the tenant pays property taxes, insurance, and maintenance on top of rent, giving the owner predictable, low-touch income.
- [Unrecaptured Section 1250 Gain](https://baker1031.com/glossary/unrecaptured-section-1250-gain/): Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain is the part of real estate gain from prior depreciation, taxed at a maximum federal rate of 25% rather than normal capital gains rates.
- [UPREIT](https://baker1031.com/glossary/upreit/): An UPREIT, or umbrella partnership REIT, is a structure that lets property owners contribute real estate for OP units, deferring tax via a 721 exchange.

## Markets by state

- [Alaska](https://baker1031.com/markets/alaska/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Alaska: no state income tax on real estate gains, plus passive DST, 721 UPREIT, and Opportunity Zone replacement options.
- [Arizona](https://baker1031.com/markets/arizona/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Arizona: a 2.5% flat income tax that conforms to Section 1031, no clawback, and Sun Belt replacement markets in Phoenix and Tucson.
- [California](https://baker1031.com/markets/california/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in California: full Section 1031 conformity, a 13.3% top rate, the FTB 3840 clawback filing, and 3.33% seller withholding explained.
- [Colorado](https://baker1031.com/markets/colorado/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Colorado: full Section 1031 conformity, a 4.4% flat rate, 2% nonresident seller withholding, and Denver-area replacement markets.
- [Hawaii](https://baker1031.com/markets/hawaii/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Hawaii: Section 1031 conformity, a 7.25% capital-gains cap, and the 7.25% HARPTA nonresident withholding explained for exchangers.
- [Idaho](https://baker1031.com/markets/idaho/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Idaho: full Section 1031 conformity, a 5.695% flat rate, no clawback or nonresident withholding, and fast-growing Boise-area markets.
- [Montana](https://baker1031.com/markets/montana/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Montana: Section 1031 conformity, a 4.1% long-term capital-gains rate, a clawback provision, and growing Bozeman and Billings markets.
- [Nevada](https://baker1031.com/markets/nevada/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Nevada: no state income tax on real estate gains, no clawback or withholding, and strong Las Vegas and Reno replacement markets.
- [New Mexico](https://baker1031.com/markets/new-mexico/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in New Mexico: Section 1031 conformity, a 5.9% top rate, no clawback or nonresident withholding, and Albuquerque-area replacement markets.
- [Oregon](https://baker1031.com/markets/oregon/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Oregon: Section 1031 conformity, a 9.9% top rate, the Form 24 clawback filing for out-of-state exchanges, and Portland-area markets.
- [Utah](https://baker1031.com/markets/utah/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Utah: full Section 1031 conformity, a 4.55% flat rate, no clawback or nonresident withholding, and fast-growing Salt Lake and Provo markets.
- [Washington](https://baker1031.com/markets/washington/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Washington: no income tax and real estate exempt from the state capital-gains tax, plus strong Seattle-area replacement markets.
- [Wyoming](https://baker1031.com/markets/wyoming/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Wyoming: no state income tax on real estate gains, no clawback or withholding, and a small market that favors out-of-state DSTs.
- [Oklahoma](https://baker1031.com/markets/oklahoma/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Oklahoma: Section 1031 conformity, a 4.75% top rate, a state capital-gains deduction, and value-oriented Oklahoma City and Tulsa markets.
- [Texas](https://baker1031.com/markets/texas/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Texas: no state income tax on real estate gains, no clawback or withholding, and deep Dallas, Houston, and Austin replacement markets.
- [Illinois](https://baker1031.com/markets/illinois/): How Illinois taxes 1031 exchanges: flat 4.95% rate, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. DST and 721 options for accredited Illinois investors.
- [Indiana](https://baker1031.com/markets/indiana/): Indiana and 1031 exchanges: low 3.0% flat tax, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. Passive DST and 721 replacement options for Indiana investors.
- [Iowa](https://baker1031.com/markets/iowa/): Iowa and 1031 exchanges: new 3.8% flat tax, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. DST and 721 passive replacement options for Iowa investors.
- [Kansas](https://baker1031.com/markets/kansas/): Kansas and 1031 exchanges: 5.58% top rate, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. Passive DST and 721 replacement options for Kansas investors.
- [Michigan](https://baker1031.com/markets/michigan/): Michigan and 1031 exchanges: flat 4.25% tax, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. DST and 721 passive replacement options for Michigan investors.
- [Minnesota](https://baker1031.com/markets/minnesota/): Minnesota and 1031 exchanges: high 9.85% top rate, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. Passive DST and 721 options for Minnesota investors.
- [Missouri](https://baker1031.com/markets/missouri/): Missouri now exempts individual long-term capital gains (2025). It still conforms to IRC 1031, with no nonresident withholding. DST and 721 options for investors.
- [Nebraska](https://baker1031.com/markets/nebraska/): Nebraska and 1031 exchanges: 5.2% top rate for 2025, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. Passive DST and 721 options for Nebraska investors.
- [North Dakota](https://baker1031.com/markets/north-dakota/): North Dakota and 1031 exchanges: low 2.5% top rate, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. Passive DST and 721 options for North Dakota investors.
- [Ohio](https://baker1031.com/markets/ohio/): Ohio and 1031 exchanges: 3.125% top rate for 2025 (flat 2.75% in 2026), full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. DST and 721 options for Ohio investors.
- [South Dakota](https://baker1031.com/markets/south-dakota/): South Dakota has no state income tax, so real estate gains face no state tax. A 1031 exchange defers the federal gain. DST and 721 options for SD investors.
- [Wisconsin](https://baker1031.com/markets/wisconsin/): Wisconsin and 1031 exchanges: 7.65% top rate with a 30% gains exclusion, full IRC conformity, no nonresident withholding. DST and 721 options for WI investors.
- [Alabama](https://baker1031.com/markets/alabama/): How Alabama taxes a 1031 exchange, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors trading out of active real estate into passive income.
- [Arkansas](https://baker1031.com/markets/arkansas/): How Arkansas taxes a 1031 exchange, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors moving from active real estate into passive income.
- [Florida](https://baker1031.com/markets/florida/): Florida has no state income tax, so a 1031 exchange faces only federal deferral. Explore DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors.
- [Georgia](https://baker1031.com/markets/georgia/): How Georgia taxes a 1031 exchange and its 3% nonresident withholding, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited real estate investors.
- [Kentucky](https://baker1031.com/markets/kentucky/): How Kentucky taxes a 1031 exchange, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors trading active real estate for passive income.
- [Louisiana](https://baker1031.com/markets/louisiana/): How Louisiana taxes a 1031 exchange, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors moving from active real estate into passive income.
- [Mississippi](https://baker1031.com/markets/mississippi/): How Mississippi taxes a 1031 exchange, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors trading active real estate for passive income.
- [North Carolina](https://baker1031.com/markets/north-carolina/): How North Carolina taxes a 1031 exchange, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors moving from active real estate to passive income.
- [South Carolina](https://baker1031.com/markets/south-carolina/): How South Carolina taxes a 1031 exchange and its 7% nonresident withholding, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited real estate investors.
- [Tennessee](https://baker1031.com/markets/tennessee/): Tennessee has no state income tax, so a 1031 exchange faces only federal deferral. Explore DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors.
- [Virginia](https://baker1031.com/markets/virginia/): How Virginia taxes a 1031 exchange, plus DST and 721 replacement options for accredited investors moving from active real estate into passive income.
- [West Virginia](https://baker1031.com/markets/west-virginia/): How West Virginia taxes a 1031 exchange and its 2.5% nonresident withholding, plus DST and 721 options for accredited real estate investors.
- [Connecticut](https://baker1031.com/markets/connecticut/): Selling Connecticut investment property? See how 1031 exchanges and DSTs let accredited investors defer state and federal capital gains and shift to passive income.
- [Delaware](https://baker1031.com/markets/delaware/): Selling Delaware investment property? Learn how 1031 exchanges and Delaware Statutory Trusts help accredited investors defer capital gains and move to passive income.
- [Maine](https://baker1031.com/markets/maine/): Selling Maine investment property? See how 1031 exchanges and DSTs help accredited investors defer state and federal capital gains and move into passive income.
- [Maryland](https://baker1031.com/markets/maryland/): Selling Maryland investment property? Learn how 1031 exchanges and DSTs let accredited investors defer capital gains and shift to passive income across state lines.
- [Massachusetts](https://baker1031.com/markets/massachusetts/): Selling Massachusetts investment property? See how 1031 exchanges and DSTs help accredited investors defer capital gains and move into passive income out of state.
- [New Hampshire](https://baker1031.com/markets/new-hampshire/): New Hampshire has no capital gains tax. See how 1031 exchanges and DSTs let accredited investors defer federal gain and move real estate equity into passive income.
- [New Jersey](https://baker1031.com/markets/new-jersey/): Selling New Jersey investment property? Learn how 1031 exchanges and DSTs help accredited investors defer high state and federal capital gains and gain passive income.
- [New York](https://baker1031.com/markets/new-york/): Selling New York investment property? See how 1031 exchanges and DSTs let accredited investors defer high state and federal capital gains and move to passive income.
- [Pennsylvania](https://baker1031.com/markets/pennsylvania/): Selling Pennsylvania investment property? Learn how 1031 exchanges and DSTs let accredited investors defer capital gains now that PA recognizes like-kind exchanges.
- [Rhode Island](https://baker1031.com/markets/rhode-island/): Selling Rhode Island investment property? See how 1031 exchanges and DSTs help accredited investors defer capital gains and move into passive, diversified income.
- [Vermont](https://baker1031.com/markets/vermont/): Selling Vermont investment property? Learn how 1031 exchanges and DSTs let accredited investors defer state and federal capital gains and shift to passive income.
- [Washington, D.C.](https://baker1031.com/markets/washington-dc/): Selling Washington, D.C. investment property? See how 1031 exchanges and DSTs help accredited investors defer high capital gains tax and move to passive income.
- [Atlanta, GA](https://baker1031.com/markets/atlanta-ga/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Atlanta: a logistics and multifamily hub. Georgia taxes gains at 5.39% and withholds 3% from nonresident sellers.
- [Austin, TX](https://baker1031.com/markets/austin-tx/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Austin: a tech-driven Sun Belt market. Texas has no state income tax, so an exchange faces no state capital-gains tax.
- [Charlotte, NC](https://baker1031.com/markets/charlotte-nc/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Charlotte: a banking and multifamily growth market. North Carolina conforms and taxes gains at a flat 4.25%.
- [Dallas–Fort Worth, TX](https://baker1031.com/markets/dallas-fort-worth-tx/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Dallas-Fort Worth: a top industrial and corporate market. Texas has no state income tax, so no state capital-gains tax.
- [Denver, CO](https://baker1031.com/markets/denver-co/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Denver: a diversified Front Range market. Colorado conforms and taxes gains at a flat 4.4% with 2% nonresident withholding.
- [Houston, TX](https://baker1031.com/markets/houston-tx/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Houston: energy, medical, and port-driven market. Texas has no state income tax, so no state capital-gains tax on an exchange.
- [Nashville, TN](https://baker1031.com/markets/nashville-tn/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Nashville: a healthcare and relocation growth market. Tennessee has no state income tax, so no state capital-gains tax.
- [Orlando, FL](https://baker1031.com/markets/orlando-fl/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Orlando: a tourism, medical, and logistics market. Florida has no state income tax, so no state capital-gains tax on an exchange.
- [Phoenix, AZ](https://baker1031.com/markets/phoenix-az/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Phoenix: a semiconductor and industrial boom market. Arizona conforms and taxes gains at a flat 2.5% with no withholding.
- [Raleigh–Durham, NC](https://baker1031.com/markets/raleigh-durham-nc/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Raleigh-Durham: a life-science and tech Triangle market. North Carolina conforms and taxes gains at a flat 4.25%.
- [Salt Lake City, UT](https://baker1031.com/markets/salt-lake-city-ut/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Salt Lake City: a tech and logistics crossroads market. Utah conforms and taxes gains at a flat 4.55% with no withholding.
- [Tampa, FL](https://baker1031.com/markets/tampa-fl/): 1031 exchange and DST investing in Tampa: a finance, healthcare, and logistics market. Florida has no state income tax, so no state capital-gains tax on an exchange.

## Calculators

- [Capital Gains Tax Deferral](https://baker1031.com/calculators/capital-gains/): Estimate the federal, state, depreciation-recapture, and 3.8% NIIT tax a 1031 exchange could defer when you sell investment real estate.
- [1031 Boot Calculator](https://baker1031.com/calculators/boot/): Calculate cash boot and mortgage boot in a 1031 exchange and see how much would be taxable — so you can structure the deal to defer fully.
- [Depreciation Recapture](https://baker1031.com/calculators/depreciation-recapture/): Estimate the unrecaptured Section 1250 tax (up to 25%) on the depreciation you've claimed on investment real estate before you sell.
- [Replacement Debt (Equal or Up)](https://baker1031.com/calculators/replacement-debt/): Find the minimum replacement-property value, equity to reinvest, and debt to replace so your 1031 exchange fully defers the tax.
- [45 / 180-Day Deadline](https://baker1031.com/calculators/deadline/): Enter your sale closing date to calculate your 1031 exchange 45-day identification deadline and 180-day closing deadline.
- [DST Cash Flow & Yield](https://baker1031.com/calculators/dst-yield/): Estimate the monthly and annual distributions and yield on a Delaware Statutory Trust (DST) investment for a given investment amount.
- [Cap Rate](https://baker1031.com/calculators/cap-rate/): Calculate a property's capitalization rate from its net operating income and value or purchase price.
- [Cash-on-Cash Return](https://baker1031.com/calculators/cash-on-cash/): Calculate your cash-on-cash return — annual pre-tax cash flow divided by the total cash you invested in a property.
- [After-Tax Sale Proceeds](https://baker1031.com/calculators/after-tax-proceeds/): Compare what you'd net from a taxable sale of investment real estate versus keeping the full amount working through a 1031 exchange.
- [Tax-Adjusted Yield](https://baker1031.com/calculators/tax-adjusted-yield/): Compare investments on an after-tax basis: enter a distribution yield, the portion sheltered by depreciation, and your tax rate.
